coredata.dc:title: Construction of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites for the removal of ipronidazole: Performance and degradation mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Jie
Guo, Xiao
He, Qiang
Wu, Fei
Yao, Binghua
coredata.dc:description: Development of visible-light photocatalysts for the removal of nitroimidazoles pharmaceutical residues in the environment has attracted widespread attention. In this respect, a series of nitrogen-containing carbon quantum dots N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites were hydrothermally synthesized for the disposal of ipronidazole. N-CQDs coupled with irregular square shaped InNbO4 was confirmed by XRD, FTIR, TEM and XPS. The as-prepared N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites displayed stronger visible-light absorption from 400 ​nm to 800 ​nm. Owing to photosensitization and electron transfer ability of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was better than that of InNbO4. The highest removal efficiency of ipronidazole solution was obtained in the presence of 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite. Reactive species ●O2 −, hole, ●OH and electron all took part in the reaction process, while the removal of ipronidazole was most affected by ●O2 −. Moreover, six new transformation products of ipronidazole were found during photocatalytic process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-CQDs
InNbO4
Composites
Ipronidazole
Pharmaceutical pollutant
Degradation mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of N-CQDs, InNbO4, and NCQDs/InNbO4 composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research & Development Program of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Research and Development Program of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21706207 ), and The Key Research & Development Program of Shaanxi Province ( 2017NY-165 , 2020KW-070 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, InNbO4 and N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites.
(a) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of N-CQDs and (b) UV–vis spectra of InNbO4 and N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites. Insets of (a) are photos of N-CQDs solution under natural light and UV lamp at 365 ​nm.
TEM images of (a) N-CQDs, (b) InNbO4, (c) 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite and (d) HRTEM images of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites.
XPS spectra of InNbO4 and 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite: (a) survey, (b) Nb 3d, (c) In 3d, (d) O 1s, (e) C 1s and (f) N 1s.
(a) Removal efficiency of ipronidazole with InNbO4, N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites and (b) Corresponding first-order kinetic fitting lines.
(a) Removal efficiency of ipronidazole with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite in recycling tests and (b) XRD patterns of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite before and after photocatalytic reaction.

(a) Instantaneous photocurrent response of InNbO4 and 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite and (b) Removal efficiency of ipronidazole with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite in the presence of different capture reagents.
(a) Chromatograms of ipronidazole solution during photocatalytic reaction and (b) The exemplary chromatogram recorded at 90 ​min.
Possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of ipronidazole.
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism of ipronidazole over 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite.
First-order kinetic parameters with different samples.

head.ce:title: Construction of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites for the removal of ipronidazole: Performance and degradation mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Xi'an University of Technology, 5 Jinhua South Road, Xi'an, Shaanxi, 710048, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Chemistry, Xi’an University of Technology, 5 Jinhua South Road, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710048, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Center, Xi'an Customs District, Shaanxi, 710068, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technical Center, Xi’an Customs District, Shaanxi 710068, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of visible-light photocatalysts for the removal of nitroimidazoles pharmaceutical residues in the environment has attracted widespread attention. In this respect, a series of nitrogen-containing carbon quantum dots N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites were hydrothermally synthesized for the disposal of ipronidazole. N-CQDs coupled with irregular square shaped InNbO4 was confirmed by XRD, FTIR, TEM and XPS. The as-prepared N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites displayed stronger visible-light absorption from 400 ​nm to 800 ​nm. Owing to photosensitization and electron transfer ability of N-CQDs, the photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was better than that of InNbO4. The highest removal efficiency of ipronidazole solution was obtained in the presence of 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite. Reactive species ●O2 −, hole, ●OH and electron all took part in the reaction process, while the removal of ipronidazole was most affected by ●O2 −. Moreover, six new transformation products of ipronidazole were found during photocatalytic process.

The photo-oxidative mechanism of the degradation of ipronidazole with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite was proposed. During this catalytic process, generated electrons could react with O2 to yield ●O2 −. Besides, h+ could oxidize water to produce ●OH. These active oxygen species were capable of decomposing ipronidazole.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroimidazoles are a class of antibacterial pharmaceuticals for human and veterinary purposes [1,2]. The usage of nitroimidazoles can effectively treat infectious diseases caused by bacteria and protozoa [3]. Traces of nitroimidazoles could be detected in the environment after the extensive use of these pharmaceuticals [4]. It was reported that nitroimidazoles residues have obvious deleterious effects on the aqueous environment [5]. Ipronidazole (1-methyl-2-isopropyl-5-nitroimidazole) is known as a more soluble imidazole compound, which is often used as feed additives [6]. Minimal amount of ipronidazole has direct effects on sperm, leading to infertility [7]. Since the chemical structure of ipronidazole is stable, it is difficult to be removed by conventional waste water treatment method. To the best of our knowledge, degradation mechanism of ipronidazole in aqueous environment is not presented in the published literatures. In this respect, it needs to develop an effective method to eliminate ipronidazole from water and unveil its degradation mechanism.

Photocatalytic technology has been demonstrated to be a sustainable process to solve environmental pollution issues due to its reliable and efficient safety features [8,9]. The disposal of recalcitrant pharmaceutical pollutants could be achieved with semiconductor photocatalysts in natural light or indoor light [10]. Indium niobate (InNbO4) is an attractive and stable material with excellent electronic and optical properties, which can be applied as phosphors [11], gas sensors [12,13] and photocatalysts [14]. As far as photocatalytic application is concerned, InNbO4 has shown to be effective in hydrogen generation [15], reduction of carbon dioxide [16] and photodecomposition of organic compounds [17]. Various types of InNbO4 such as nanocrystalline InNbO4 [18], InNbO4 nanofibers [19], graphene/InNbO4 [20] and N-doped InNbO4 [21] have been widely applied in the field of photocatalysis. Owing to its narrowed optical absorption region and easier recombination of electron-hole pairs, the performance of InNbO4 photocatalysts is still limited under visible-light irradiation.

In recent times, nitrogen-containing carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), as a promising candidate of quantum dots have drawn great interest in bioimaging [22], fluorescent detection [23], electrocatalysis [24] and photocatalysis [25]. Unlike carbon quantum dots, N-CQDs bearing nitrogen atoms could provide more active sites, thus affecting its optical and electronic properties. In view of superior up-conversion and electron transfer performance, N-CQDs was used to modify traditional photocatalysts. For instance, N-CQDs/Bi2WO6 [26], N-CQDs/Ag3PO4 [27], N-CQDs/BiOBr [28] and N-CQDs/Bi4O5I2 [29] have been reported. The superior photocatalytic performance of these modified photocatalysts was attributed to visible-light sensitive and electron transfer ability of N-CQDs. Based on the merits of N-CQDs, we expect that modification with N-CQDs will improve visible-light reactivity of InNbO4.

Herein, N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was prepared through a facile hydrothermal method. InNbO4 modified with different N-CQDs ratios affected the physicochemical properties of the synthesized materials were tested. Under visible-light illumination, N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was applied to decompose veterinary medicine ipronidazole. The possible photo-oxidative mechanism of ipronidazole over N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was elucidated in detail.

In(NO3)3·4.5H2O, ethanol, p-benzoquinone (BQ), disodium edetate (EDTA), tert-butanol (TBA) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent company. NbCl5 and Ipronidazole standard (>99%) were obtained from Alfa Aesar Company. Ecuadorian shrimp was purchased from Walmart supermarket (Xi'an, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade. Laboratory water is deionized water.

At first, N-CQDs were synthesized through a green hydrothermal process using Ecuadorian shrimp shell as starting materials [30]. InNbO4 nanoparticles were prepared with In(NO3)3·4.5H2O and NbCl5 in ethanol solution according to our previous developed solvothermal method [31]. Further, suitable amount of N-CQDs and 0.20 ​g of InNbO4 catalyst were dispersed in 25 ​mL of deionized. Adjust the pH of the mixture to 1.5 with dilute hydrochloric acid. Stir the obtained mixture for 1 ​h. The precursor solution was then transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 180 ​°C for 12 ​h. After the reaction was complete, the suspension was filtered and dried. Finally, the obtained composites were denoted as 1 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4, 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4, 5 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 and 7 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 in accordance with the mass ratio of N-CQDs versus InNbO4.

The crystal structure of the sample was analyzed by XRD-7000s X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation scanning from 10° to 70°. TEM and HRTEM of the sample were observed by JEM-3010 using an acceleration voltage of 300 ​kV. The formation of the composites was determined by a FT-IR 8900 instrument. The chemical state of the composites was measured with a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were performed on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer using BaSO4 as reference. Thermogravimetry experiment was conducted on a TA Q600 instrument at heating rate of 10 ​°C/min. ICP measurement was carried out on an iCAP 6000 Series. A CHI 660b electrochemical workstation was used to monitor the photocurrent of the samples. 0.1 ​mol/L of Na2SO4 electrolyte was used in the test. The prepared sample modified on ITO, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively.

The photocatalytic performance of the prepared catalysts was carried out in a self-made reactor. A 250 ​W xenon lamp located in a pyrex circulating condenser was used as visible-light source. For the elimination of UV light, a UV Cut-Off filter (<420 ​nm) was placed outside the circulating condenser. The distance between light source and reaction solution was 10 ​cm. In a typical process, 75 ​mg of the catalysts was dispersed in 50 ​mL of ipronidazole solution (10 ​mg/L). To achieve adsorption and desorption equilibrium of the reaction mixture, test tubes were pumped with air in dark for 30 ​min. Then, turn on the light and withdraw samples from the test tubes every 30 ​min. The samples were filtered through a 0.22 ​μm membrane. The remaining solution was monitored by a Dionex Ultimate 3000 UHPLC system with aQ ExactiveTM hybrid Quadrupole-Orbitrap HRMS and a HESI-II detector. More specific parameter about UHPLC/MS/MS method is provided in the supporting information. The removal efficiency (R) of ipronidazole was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
R
( % )
=
(
1 −
C t
C 0
)
× 100 %
Where R is the removal efficiency of ipronidazole, c0 is the initial concentration of ipronidazole solution and c t is the concentration of ipronidazole solution at time t.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CQDs, InNbO4, and NCQDs/InNbO4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure of the prepared N-CQDs, InNbO4, InNbO4 with different ratios of N-CQDs were analyzed by powder XRD. As seen in Fig. 1 a, an amorphous peak appeared around 20° was ascribed to (002) crystal plane of N-CQDs, which was related to highly disordered carbon atoms [32] and relatively small particle size of the sample [33]. The XRD peaks of pure InNbO4 located at 24.06°, 29.68°, 30.96°, 34.68°, 35.64°, 37.44°, 42.72°, 47.12°, 49.36°, 51.04°, 51.90°, 52.60°, 59.10°, 62.82° and 67.28°, were well matched with the standard card (JCPDS 33–0619). Moreover, four synthesized N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites and pure InNbO4 have similar XRD patterns. The characteristic peak of N-CQDs did not appear, which might be due to low content of N-CQDs in the prepared N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites [34]. Owing to the acidic and high temperature hydrothermal reaction condition, the dissolution of indium species might happen (Table. S1). As a result [111] and other peaks in N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites showed lower intensity than that of in pure InNbO4. To further investigate the formation of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites, FTIR was used to analyze the prepared samples. As shown in Fig. 1b, infrared spectra of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites possessed both characteristic peaks of N-CQDs and InNbO4. FTIR Bands appeared at 2350 ​cm−1 and 658 ​cm−1 in InNbO4 and N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites were attributed to CC stretching vibration and CC bending vibration, respectively. Vibration bands located at 1652 ​cm−1 in N-CQDs and N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites belonged to the stretching vibration of CN [12], indicating that N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was formed successfully.

The optical properties of N-CQDs, InNbO4 and N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites were recorded by UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the UV absorption peak of N-CQDs was 276 ​nm, suggesting CC and C–N structure of N-CQDs [12,35]. At an excitation wavelength of 367 ​nm, the PL peak was observed at 440 ​nm. Also, the prepared N-CQDs solution displayed pale yellow in natural light and bright blue under UV lamp at 365 ​nm, which is more suitable for photocatalytic application. As displayed in Fig. 2b, pure InNbO4 exhibited UV–vis absorption in the range of 200–400 ​nm, reflecting electronic transition state of InNbO4. This also indicated that InNbO4 sample displayed weak absorption in visible light region. Furthermore, the prepared N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites showed enhanced absorption from 400 ​nm to 800 ​nm to the aid of N-CQDs. Stronger visible light absorption capacity of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites can be attributed to photosensitization of N-CQDs [36]. Among four N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites, 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite displayed strongest absorption towards visible light. This result showed that modification of InNbO4 with suitable amount of N-CQDs could expand visible light absorption range of InNbO4 to some extent.

The morphological characteristics of N-CQDs, InNbO4 and 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite was observed through TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the synthesized N-CQDs with an average size of 4 ​nm were highly dispersed. The pure InNbO4 in Fig. 3b exhibited irregular square shaped images with the sizes of 500–600 ​nm. After a combination of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs and InNbO4 through hydrothermal treatment, InNbO4 surrounded by N-CQDs could be clearly seen in Fig. 3c. The corresponding HRTEM image of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite was shown in Fig. 3d. The lattice distances of 0.30 and 0.25 ​nm was assigned to the (002) lattice plane of N-CQDs and the (120) lattice plane InNbO4, indicating N-CQDs coexisted with InNbO4 in the composite.

The element composition and valence state of InNbO4 and 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite was analyzed by XPS. In the survey spectrum (Fig. 4 a), XPS peaks of In, Nb and O were present in both samples. Besides, XPS peak of N element could be seen in 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite. This indicated that N-CQDs was combined with InNbO4 successfully. To verify the valence state of each element, high resolution XPS spectra was obtained. As seen in Fig. 4b, two peaks located at 205.9 and 208.6 ​eV belonged to Nb 3d5/2 and Nb 3d3/2, respectively. This confirms that niobium exists in Nb5+ [15]. In Fig. 4c, two strong peaks located at 442.9 and 450.3 ​eV were assigned to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, indicating In element could be expressed as In3+ [37]. As shown in Fig. 4d, XPS spectra of O 1s displayed a lattice oxygen peak at 530.6 ​eV. In Fig. 4e, the fitting XPS spectra of C 1s showed two peaks at 282.8 ​eV and 286.3eV, which could be ascribed to C–C and C–N functional groups, respectively [12,38]. As shown in Fig. 4f, the XPS peak of N 1s at 398.1 ​eV might be attributed to pyridinic-N [39]. Considering XPS data of Nb 3d (Fig. 4b), In 3d (Fig. 4c) and O1s (Fig. 4d) in pure InNbO4, the corresponding data of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite were all shifted to higher binding energies. This suggested that N-CQDs might combine chemically to form N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite. The content of N-CQDs in 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite was further determined by TG analyses. As displayed in Fig. S1, 3.18 ​wt% of N-CQDs deposited on InNbO4 was confirmed.

The addition of N-CQDs to InNbO4 nanomaterials will influence the photocatalytic properties of the prepared samples. In this regard, the degradation efficiency of ipronidazole solution with different samples was analyzed in detail. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the photocatalytic performance of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites were better than that of pure InNbO4 nanomaterials. The modification of InNbO4 could improve % removal of ipronidazole. More specifically, the removal efficiency of ipronidazole solution was 75.7%, 81.4%, 97.2%, 86.6% and 88.3% with pure InNbO4, 1 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4, 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4, 5 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 and 7 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4, respectively. The first-order kinetic equation was used to fit the tendency of the rate of degradation in the presence of different samples. The obtained results were shown in Fig. 5b and Table 1 . With the increase of N-CQDs ratio from 1 ​wt% to 7 ​wt% in N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites, the % removal and K 1 of ipronidazole solution increased at first, but dropped off at higher N-CQDs ratios. At the beginning, the increasement of degradation rate might be attributed to carrier migration from N-CQDs to InNbO4, thus prolonging photo-generated electrons-hole life span. However, too many N-CQDs on the surface of InNbO4 might block enough light to be absorbed by N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites [40]. This causes the decrease in degradation rate of ipronidazole solution. Therefore, 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites with the highest degradation rate of ipronidazole was selected for further study.

Reusability embodies the adaptability of catalysts in practical application. In this respect, repeated photocatalytic process with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was performed. As shown in Fig. 6 a, after 5 repeated usages of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites, the removal efficiency of ipronidazole can still reach 94.3%. Compared with the original XRD pattern of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites (Fig. 6b), only the XRD intensity of used 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was slightly lower, suggesting the crystal structure of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites remained the same. The results indicated that 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites could maintain its stability to some extent after waste water treatment.

To evaluate the separation efficiency of photoelectron-hole pairs, the instantaneous photocurrent response of InNbO4 and 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite were performed on an electrochemical system. During the experiment, 10 ​mg of the sample was dissolved in ethanol and subsequently coated on ITO surface. The photocurrent generation of the two working electrodes was determined under intermittent Xenon lamp irradiation. As presented in Fig. 7 a, the photocurrent generation of InNbO4 and 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite increased rapidly when light was on and lasted for 100 ​s. During the next period, the photocurrent generation was decreased. In the course of time, the two samples displayed similar photocurrent behaviors. Moreover, the photocurrent value of 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite was 2.5 times greater than that of InNbO4. This revealed that better photoelectron-hole pairs separation efficiency might exist in 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite. As we all know, the catalytic activity of photocatalysts is closely related to the separation efficiency of photoelectron-hole pairs [41]. Thus, the 3 ​wt % N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite of better photocatalytic performance was achieved.

To elucidate possible active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of ipronidazole solution in the presence of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites, BQ, EDTA, TBA and DMSO were used as ●O2 −, hole, ●OH and electron trapping agents, respectively. The removal efficiency of ipronidazole solution with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites by the addition of different scavengers was displayed in Fig. 7b. As can be seen, % removal of ipronidazole was significantly reduced from 97.2% to 34.2% in the presence of BQ, suggesting ●O2 − was main active oxygen species in the reaction. While EDTA, TBA and DMSO was brought into the reaction solution, % removal of ipronidazole was reduced to 67.9%, 74.9% and 73.9%, respectively. This indicates that hole, ●OH and electron also act as active species for ipronidazole degradation. It was reported that ●O2 − was produced by the reaction of O2 with e‒ (Eq. (2)). Most of ●OH was coming from the oxidation of water with hole (Eq. (3)) [42].
(2) O2 ​+ ​e‒ → ●O2 −
(3) H2O ​+ ​h+ → ●OH ​+ ​H+
This result suggested that ●O2 −, hole, ●OH and electron all took part in the reaction process, while the removal of ipronidazole was most affected by ●O2 −.

The photocatalytic degradation of ipronidazole is a complicated reaction. Examining the transformation products of ipronidazole under photocatalytic process is important for elucidating degradation pathways. In this experiment, UHPLC/MS/MS analyses were conducted to detect the degradation products in the case of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites. The solutions of ipronidazole were determined at 0, 30, 60, 90, 150 and 180 ​min during photocatalytic reaction. The chromatogram recorded for the reaction solutions of ipronidazole is presented in Fig. 8 . As seen in Fig. 8a, the signal of ipronidazole and its transformation products was properly displayed. During the established photocatalytic process, the concentration of ipronidazole (R.T. 3.55 ​min) decreased with time. Moreover, six transformation products of ipronidazole were also presented. To see the transformation products more clearly, the exemplary chromatogram recorded at 90 ​min was shown in Fig. 8b. Compound I (m/z 170.09, R.T. 3.08 ​min), Compound II (m/z 126.12, R.T. 2.45 ​min), Compound III (m/z 170.09, R.T. 1.75 ​min), Compound IV (m/z 88.06, R.T. 1.36 ​min), Compound V (m/z 74.06, R.T. 1.20 ​min) and Compound VI (m/z 101.11, R.T. 1.10 ​min), as well as ipronidazole (m/z 170.09, R.T. 3.55 ​min) were found. The corresponding mass spectrometry for these signals were presented in Fig. S2. According to retention time and mass data of each compound, the chemical structure of Compound I, Compound II, Compound III, Compound IV, Compound V and VI could be elucidated. Also, possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of ipronidazole with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was summarized in Scheme 1 . During the photocatalytic reaction, the rearrangement of ipronidazole took place first. This resulted in the formation of Compound I (m/z 170.09, R.T. 3.08 ​min). Compound I might be converted to its tautomeric Compound III (m/z 170.09, R.T. 1.75 ​min). Similar rearrangement in dimetridazole, metronidazole, ornidazole, secnidazole and tinidazole under photolysis has been reported [43]. Compound II (m/z 126.12, R.T. 2.45 ​min) produced from denitration of ipronidazole was identified. Followed by deisopropyl and demethylation, Compound IV (m/z 88.06, R.T. 1.36 ​min) was produced. At the same time, Compound VI (m/z 101.11, R.T. 1.10 ​min) was formed through demethylation and imidazole ring opening. Subsequently, Compound VI could be further degraded to Compound V (m/z 74.06, R.T. 1.20 ​min) via dimethylamine and demethylation. Therefore, six new transformation products were identified and the reaction pathway of ipronidazole in the presence of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was confirmed.

Based on the above results, the photo-oxidative mechanism of the degradation of ipronidazole with 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was proposed and displayed in Scheme 2 . Under 250 ​W xenon lamp illumination, e‒ could be excited from valence band to conduction band and produce h+ on the surface of InNbO4. Due to weak visible light absorption ability and easier recombination of electron-hole pairs, the removal efficiency of ipronidazole was relatively low. With the addition of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs to InNbO4, stronger visible light absorption ability and fast carrier delocalization of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites could be achieved. This is favorable for photocatalytic degradation of ipronidazole. Moreover, the presence of pyridine-N might produce negative charge around N-CQDs, which is beneficial to the adsorption of O2 [44]. The generated electrons could react with O2 to yield ●O2 −. Besides, h+ could oxidize water to produce ●OH. These active oxygen species were capable of decomposing ipronidazole. The formation of six transformation products of ipronidazole was identified. As a result, ipronidazole pollutant could be removed to some extent in the presence of 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites.

In this work, N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites with N-CQDs weight ratios of 1 ​wt%, 3 ​wt%, 5 ​wt% and 7 ​wt% were successfully synthesized by a facile hydrothermal route. The formation of N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was determined by XRD, FT-IR, TEM and XPS techniques. Incorporating 3 ​wt% NCQDs into InNbO4 could enhance its visible-light absorption and electron transfer attributes. The as-prepared N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites was applied to degrade ipronidazole pollutant. The photocatalytic disposal of ipronidazole solution fitted well with first-order kinetic equation. Under 250 ​W xenon lamp irradiation, the highest ipronidazole removal efficiency of 97.2% was achieved by 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites. The as-prepared 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites showed good stability after five successive usages. The involvement of ●O2 −, hole, ●OH and electron might account for the degradation of ipronidazole, while ●O2 − was predominant reactive species. Moreover, six new transformation products of ipronidazole were found. The results revealed that 3 ​wt% N-CQDs/InNbO4 composites could be applied as an efficient visible-light photocatalysts for the removal of environmental pollutants.
All data generated or used during the study appear in the submitted article.

Jie Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiao Guo: Investigation, Data curation. Qiang He: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Fei Wu: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Binghua Yao: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21706207), and The Key Research & Development Program of Shaanxi Province (2017NY-165, 2020KW-070).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssc.2021.122567.
